title: The TRUTH about Sakana Fugu..
summary_verdict: Sakana Fugu validates that learned orchestration is a real product category — Vercel shipped it day one — but the model is not a base-model replacement, and Sakana's evaluation methodology is currently not credible enough to trust the published numbers.
summary_key_takeaways: Use Fugu as a planner inside a slow-planner / fast-executor loop, not as a drop-in replacement for Claude, Gemini, or Qwen.
Wait for Sakana to publish named-baseline comparisons and token/cost reporting before trusting any Fugu benchmark — the current anonymized `Model A, B, C` setup is insufficient.
If you ship any orchestration or routing layer in 2026, disclose cost, routing overhead, and compare against parallel voting, multi-agent debate, or self-consistency — not just base models.
Adopt learned orchestration if you already run multi-step agent workflows and manually switch models; skip it if you make single monolithic LLM calls.
Treat Vercel adding `Fugu Ultra` to its AI Gateway on day one as a demand signal, not a quality signal — infrastructure adoption and benchmark credibility are separate questions.
Ignore Fugu's overall `SWE-bench Pro` score and instead measure it against your own harness on your own long-horizon tasks, since it trails `Claude Opus` by ~10 points on that benchmark.
Re-evaluate any Sakana-published performance numbers with extra skepticism given Blanche Minerva's challenge about prior "impossible performance claims in earlier work."

summary_key_takeaways: Run Fugu Ultra on Codex with `high` reasoning effort instead of `xhigh` to fit ~4 tasks inside the 5-hour window.
Use a local Windows machine with a discrete GPU for any 3D/WebGL task — a 4 GB VPS cannot drive the cyberpunk neon output.
Prefer the GLM 5.2 + Kimi 2.7 spec pipeline over Fugu Ultra for landing-page design work, even though Fugu has web search and vision.
Attach a PRD, context files, and offline-vs-CDN constraints to every Fugu prompt to avoid the default-template fallback seen on the hero image.
Move Fugu Ultra off Codex and into Pi or Droid to drop token costs, per the community tip in the video.
Skip `xhigh` on Codex unless a single task needs it — one prompt already consumed 84% of the 5-hour limit.
Hold off on Fugu default until the next video publishes; benchmark scores trail Fugu Ultra everywhere except SWE-bench Pro.

title: Gemini Agent vs Hermes Agent (NEW Google Interactions API)
summary_verdict: The harness is now the product, not the model — Google's polished vertical stack (Interactions API, Antfarm sandbox, installable skills) beats open-source on safety and ergonomics, but Hermes Agent's 200K-star DIY route is the pick if you need cross-machine handoffs and GUI control today.
summary_key_takeaways: Use Google's Interactions API as the single endpoint for both Gemini model calls and agent invocations instead of wiring up separate SDKs for chat vs. tool execution.
Route any agent that writes files or executes code through the `antfarm` remote Linux sandbox rather than a local downloads folder or system path.
Install the Gemini interaction skills package on your coding agent so it learns the current SDK patterns and model versions before you migrate.

Switch to Hermes Agent for GUI control on Windows and Linux if you need the AI to click and type inside existing desktop apps, but verify UI-element targeting per app — Hermes still guesses locations.

Skip cross-device agent handoff workflows until a discovery protocol standardizes; today the nine open-source communication protocols do not agree on how agents find each other across decentralized systems.
Benchmark your choice by `200K GitHub stars` and weekly commit cadence on Hermes, not by Gemini 2.5 vs. Claude 4 leaderboard scores — the model is the engine, the harness is the product.

Treat the Logan Kilpatrick promotion of Interactions API to GA as the trigger to consolidate your tool wiring into one endpoint instead of maintaining parallel stacks for Gemini and open-source agents.
